title: <ul>
content:
• Opening: This is your time to hook the readers. Think about why they should be interested in your story. Start with why the story is interesting to you! 
  • Narrative Paragraphs: Break down the event or story into smaller pieces or paragraphs. Make these paragraphs simple explanations of what happened. You'll want to make each narrative portion interesting to the reader. Think about times of suspense, imagery, or surprise, along with a bit of humor.
  • Closing: You don't need to hit your reader over the head with a summary of the event! But many narratives end with a lesson or moral.
Remember, there are no right or wrong concepts or ideas in prewriting. Don't limit yourself. Let your ideas flow. Use the samples from your e-book or this week's lesson to guide your writing.
Perform the following tasks to complete your assignment:
Complete the reading assignment and the lecture before attempting this assignment. When completing the reading assignment, pay close attention to the brainstorming strategies discussed in your book:
    • Freewriting
    • Mapping/Clustering
    • Brainstorming/Listing
    • Questioning
